title: The Passive and Impersonal Constructions
description: Spanish has several ways to say what English packs into the be-passive. Meet the formal ser-passive (with an agreeing participle), the everyday pasiva refleja with se, the impersonal se, and the crucial estar + participle (a resulting state) versus ser + participle (the action). Learn which one Spanish actually prefers.

cards.front: La casa fue construida por los romanos.
cards.back: ser-passive: ser + past participle (+ por + agent); participle AGREES
cards.notes: The formal passive = **ser** + past participle. Here the participle behaves like an adjective and **agrees** in gender and number with the subject: *La casa fue **construida***, *Los puentes fueron **construidos***. The agent (the doer) is introduced by **por**: *por los romanos*. This sounds bookish or journalistic; in speech Spanish usually avoids it.

cards.front: Los libros fueron escritos / La carta fue escrita
cards.back: in the ser-passive the participle matches the subject (gender + number)
cards.notes: Contrast this with compound tenses (*haber* + participle), where the participle is **frozen**: *He **escrito** las cartas* (no agreement). In the ser-passive it must agree: *Las cartas **fueron escritas***. English speakers often forget to make it agree because English participles never change.

cards.front: Se venden coches. / Se vende coche.
cards.back: pasiva refleja: se + verb, agreeing with the grammatical subject
cards.notes: The much more common passive uses **se** + a 3rd-person verb that **agrees** with the thing affected: *Se **vende** un coche* (singular) vs *Se **venden** coches* (plural). Think of it as 'cars get sold'. No agent is normally expressed.

cards.front: Se habla español. / Se hablan varios idiomas.
cards.back: the se-passive verb is singular or plural to match its subject
cards.notes: The verb tracks the **number of the subject**: one thing → singular (*Se habla español*), several things → plural (*Se hablan varios idiomas*). A classic English-speaker error is leaving the verb singular for a plural subject: *Se vende coches* should be **Se venden coches**.

cards.front: Se vive bien aquí. / Se dice que va a llover.
cards.back: impersonal se: no specific subject, verb always 3rd person SINGULAR
cards.notes: When there is **no subject at all** ('one', 'people', 'they' in general), use **se** + verb in the **3rd person singular**: *Se **vive** bien aquí* (one lives well here), *Se **dice** que...* (they say that...). Unlike the pasiva refleja, this verb never goes plural.

cards.front: Se venden libros. (passive) vs Se trabaja mucho. (impersonal)
cards.back: with a grammatical subject the verb agrees; with none it stays singular
cards.notes: Quick test: is there a noun the verb can agree with? *Se venden **libros*** → yes, *libros* is the subject, so plural. *Se trabaja mucho* → no noun subject, so it stays **singular** (impersonal). Intransitive verbs like *vivir, trabajar, estar* are almost always impersonal.

cards.back: estar + participle = the RESULT/state of an action (not the action)
cards.notes: **estar** + past participle describes a **resulting state**: *La puerta **está cerrada*** = the door is (in the state of being) closed. The participle agrees like an adjective. This is not really a passive — it tells you how things *are now*, not who did what.

cards.front: La puerta fue cerrada (action) vs La puerta está cerrada (state)
cards.back: ser + participle = the action happening; estar + participle = the state after
cards.notes: *La puerta **fue cerrada** (por el guardia)* focuses on the **event** of closing it. *La puerta **está cerrada*** focuses on the **state** afterwards. English uses 'is closed' for both, which is why learners mix them up. Ask: action (ser) or result (estar)?

cards.front: El libro fue publicado. → Se publicó el libro.
cards.back: Spanish usually prefers the se-passive (or an active sentence) over ser
cards.notes: Where English reaches for the be-passive, natural Spanish prefers the **pasiva refleja** (*Se publicó el libro*) or simply an **active** sentence (*Publicaron el libro*). Reserve the **ser**-passive for formal or written style, especially when you want to name the agent with *por*.

cards.front: It is said that... → Se dice que... (NOT *Es dicho que...*)
cards.back: do not translate every English be-passive word-for-word
cards.notes: English loves the passive ('it is said', 'Spanish is spoken here', 'cars are sold'). Translating each literally with *ser* sounds wrong: say **Se dice que**, **Aquí se habla español**, **Se venden coches**. The instinct to use *ser* for every be-passive is the #1 trap here.

cards.front: Han construido la casa. (no agreement) vs La casa fue construida. (agreement)
cards.back: compound-tense participle is invariable; ser-passive participle agrees
cards.notes: Don't let agreement bleed across. After **haber** the participle is **invariable**: *Han **construido** las casas* (never *construidas*). In the **ser**-passive it **agrees**: *Las casas fueron **construidas***. Same word, opposite rule depending on the helper verb.

cards.back: name the doer with por in the ser-passive
cards.notes: When you do want to say **who** performed the action, the agent follows **por**: *El cuadro fue pintado **por Picasso***. The pasiva refleja normally can't add an agent, so naming the doer is one of the few good reasons to choose the ser-passive.

steps.title: Two passives and an impersonal
steps.body:
# The Passive and Impersonal Constructions

English reaches for the **be-passive** constantly: *the house was built*, *Spanish is spoken here*, *cars are sold*. Spanish has the same idea but usually expresses it differently — and overusing the literal *ser*-passive is the classic English-speaker mistake.

You have three main tools:

## 1. The ser-passive (formal)

**ser** + past participle, with the participle **agreeing** like an adjective:

- *La casa **fue construida**.* (the house was built)
- *Los puentes **fueron construidos**.* (plural → -os)
- *La carta **fue escrita**.* (feminine → -a)

To name the doer, use **por**:

> *América **fue descubierta por** Colón.*

This sounds bookish. In everyday speech Spanish avoids it.

## 2. The pasiva refleja (everyday)

Far more common: **se** + a 3rd-person verb that **agrees** with the thing affected:

- *Se **vende** un coche.* (singular subject → singular verb)
- *Se **venden** coches.* (plural subject → plural verb)
- *Se **habla** español aquí.*

Think 'cars **get** sold'. Normally no agent is mentioned.

steps.title: Impersonal se vs the se-passive
steps.body:
## 3. The impersonal se

When there is **no subject at all** — 'one', 'people', 'they' in general — use **se** + verb in the **3rd person singular**, always:

- *Se **vive** bien aquí.* (one lives well here)
- *Se **dice** que va a llover.* (they say it's going to rain)
- *Se **trabaja** mucho en esta empresa.*

## Telling them apart

Both start with *se*. The difference is whether a noun can be the **grammatical subject**:

| Sentence | Is there a noun subject? | Verb |
|---|---|---|
| Se venden **libros**. | yes (*libros*) | plural → *venden* |
| Se vende **un libro**. | yes (*un libro*) | singular → *vende* |
| Se vive bien. | no | singular → *vive* |
| Se dice que... | no | singular → *dice* |

> The #1 agreement error: *Se vende coches* for plural. With *coches* (plural subject) it must be **Se venden coches**.

steps.title: ser vs estar + participle, and what to prefer
steps.body:
## Action vs result: ser vs estar + participle

English 'the door is closed' is ambiguous. Spanish splits it:

- **ser** + participle = the **action** taking place: *La puerta **fue cerrada** (por el guardia).* — someone closed it.
- **estar** + participle = the **resulting state**: *La puerta **está cerrada**.* — it is now closed.

In both, the participle agrees: *Las ventanas **están cerradas***.

## Agreement warning

The ser-passive participle agrees, **but the compound-tense participle does not**:

- *Han **construido** las casas.* (after *haber* → invariable)
- *Las casas **fueron construidas**.* (ser-passive → agrees)

## Which to choose

Where English uses the be-passive, natural Spanish prefers:

1. the **pasiva refleja**: *Se publicó el libro.*
2. or a plain **active** sentence: *Publicaron el libro.*

Keep the **ser**-passive for formal/written style — above all when you want to name the agent with **por**: *El cuadro **fue pintado por** Picasso.*

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish sentence with the construction it uses.

pairs.left: La casa fue construida por los romanos.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the little word that turns this into a pasiva refleja.
exercise.hint: The everyday passive uses this reflexive-looking particle, not ser.

exercise.prompt: Fill in vender so it agrees with its plural subject coches.
exercise.sentence: En esa tienda se ___ coches de segunda mano.
exercise.hint: The subject coches is plural, so the verb must be plural too (not vende).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *The house was built by the Romans.* (ser-passive, participle agrees, agent with por)
accept: La casa fue construida por los romanos.
la casa fue construida por los romanos
La casa fue construida por los Romanos.
La casa fue construida por los romanos
exercise.hint: ser (fue) + construir; construida agrees with la casa (feminine singular); agent = por los romanos.
distractors: La casa fue construido por los romanos.
La casa estuvo construida por los romanos.
La casa fue construida de los romanos.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Spanish is spoken here.* (use the pasiva refleja, not ser)
exercise.hint: se + hablar (3rd singular, español is singular); do NOT use es hablado.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence describes the resulting STATE 'The door is closed'? (estar, not ser)
exercise.hint: A resulting state uses estar; the participle agrees with la puerta (feminine).

steps.title: Build: They say it's going to rain

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *They say (that) it's going to rain.* (impersonal se)
exercise.hint: Impersonal se + decir in the 3rd person singular: Se dice que...

exercise.prompt: Fill in vivir for the impersonal se (no subject, 3rd person singular).
exercise.sentence: En este pueblo se ___ muy bien.
exercise.hint: No noun subject here, so the verb stays singular: se vive.

exercise.prompt: Say 'The book was published' the natural, everyday way using the pasiva refleja (preterite of publicar).
exercise.hint: se + publicar in the preterite, agreeing with el libro (singular): se publicó.

exercise.prompt: Match each sentence with whether it names the action (ser) or the resulting state (estar).
Fill in the participle of construir after han (compound tense → invariable).
exercise.sentence: Los obreros han ___ las casas en un año.
exercise.hint: After haber the participle never changes: construido (not construidas).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *America was discovered by Columbus.* (ser-passive with agent)
accept: América fue descubierta por Cristóbal Colón.
exercise.hint: ser (fue) + descubrir; descubierta agrees with América (feminine); agent = por Colón.
